content.problemStatement:
Consider a binary array B=B1,B2,…,BMB = B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_MB=B1​,B2​,…,BM​ of length MMM. Being a binary array means that every BiB_iBi​ is either 000 or 111. 

Score(B)Score(B)Score(B) is defined as the number of indices iii (((where 1≤i<M)1 \leq i < M)1≤i<M) such that Bi≠Bi+1B_i \neq B_{i+1}Bi​=Bi+1​.

You are given a binary array A=A1,A2,…,ANA = A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA=A1​,A2​,…,AN​ of length NNN. Print a binary array CCC of length NNN such that


A≠CA \neq CA=C, and
Score(A)=Score(C)Score(A)=Score(C)Score(A)=Score(C).
That is, print a binary array CCC which is not identical to AAA, but has the same Score as that of AAA.

It can be proven that such a CCC always exists.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input:

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the length of the binary array AAA.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ representing the binary array AAA.




Output Format
For each test case, print NNN space-separated integers C1,C2,…,CNC_1, C_2, \ldots, C_NC1​,C2​,…,CN​ satisfying all the given conditions.


If there are multiple solutions, you can print any of them.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the length of the binary array. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1 … AN, each being 0 or 1.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a line with N space‑separated integers C1 … CN forming a binary array C such that C ≠ A and Score(C) = Score(A). Any valid array may be printed.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case N=1, A=[0]. Complement is [1] which has Score 0 and differs from A. For the second test case A=[1,1,0]; complement is [0,0,1] which also has exactly one transition (between positions 2 and 3) and is different from A. Any other valid array would also be accepted.

solution.approach: Observe that flipping every bit of the array (replacing 0 with 1 and 1 with 0) does not change whether two adjacent elements are equal or different. Therefore the number of indices i with Bi ≠ Bi+1 (the Score) stays exactly the same. The complemented array is always different from the original one because a binary array cannot be equal to its own complement. Hence for each test case we simply output the bitwise complement of A.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the length of the array. Over all test cases the total time is O(∑N).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space besides the input storage (output can be printed on the fly).

code.Python3:
import sys

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        line = []
        for _ in range(N):
            x = int(next(it))
            line.append(str(1 - x))
        out_lines.append(' '.join(line))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
